%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Lowpass Filters

> **Section Objective:** Learn how lowpass filters are designed by placing poles so low frequencies are passed and high frequencies are attenuated.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- First-order lowpass pole
- Magnitude as distance effect
- Ideal lowpass response
- Wall of poles
- Butterworth order N
- Butterworth vs Chebyshev

## 1. First-order lowpass: one pole creates high gain near \(\omega=0\)

A lowpass filter must have its maximum gain at zero frequency. To achieve this, the pole is placed on the negative real axis, directly opposite the origin of the \(j\omega\)-axis.

A pole enhances gain for frequencies near it. Since the pole sits close to the origin, points \(j\omega\) near \(\omega=0\) are the closest to the pole and therefore receive the strongest gain boost.

**Minimal example:** If the pole is at \(-\omega_c\), then points \(j\omega\) near the origin are closer to the pole than high-frequency points are. As \(\omega\) grows, the evaluation point moves up the imaginary axis and away from the pole, so gain falls.

### KEY INSIGHT

Pole placement determines which frequencies are favored. For lowpass behavior, place the pole near the low-frequency region of the \(j\omega\)-axis.

## 2. Magnitude falls because the distance to the pole grows

This is the textbook's first-order lowpass transfer function.

- \(s\) is the complex frequency variable.
- \(\omega_c\) is the cutoff-frequency parameter; it also sets the pole location at \(s = -\omega_c\).
- The single pole appears in the denominator at \(-\omega_c\) on the negative real axis.

**When to use it:** When the pole-zero diagram shows one real pole at \(-\omega_c\) and the dc gain is normalized to unity.

**Exam trigger:** 'One-pole lowpass with unity dc gain.'

**Common misuse:** Forgetting the numerator \(\omega_c\). Without it, \(H(0) = 1/\omega_c \neq 1\), destroying the normalization.

$$H(s)=\frac{\omega_c}{s+\omega_c}$$

## 3. Ideal lowpass needs a wall of poles

The numerator \(\omega_c\) was chosen precisely so that the dc gain equals unity: substituting \(s=0\) gives \(H(0)=\omega_c/\omega_c=1\).

**dc** means \(\omega=0\), i.e., a constant (zero-frequency) input signal.

**Common misuse:** Assuming a lowpass filter always has unity dc gain without checking whether the numerator is normalized. A different numerator constant would shift the dc gain away from 1.

$$H(0)=1$$


## 4. Butterworth versus Chebyshev: what the textbook wants you to remember

The frequency response is evaluated on the imaginary axis at \(s=j\omega\). The textbook uses the distance \(d\) from the fixed pole at \(-\omega_c\) to the evaluation point \(j\omega\).

As \(\omega\) increases, the evaluation point moves upward along the imaginary axis. The distance \(d\) from that moving point back to the fixed pole grows larger. Because gain is proportional to \(1/d\), a larger distance means smaller gain.

### COMMON MISTAKE

> Do not say high frequency is reduced because the pole moves. **The pole stays fixed.** The point \(j\omega\) moves.

This distinction matters for exam questions that ask you to explain the mechanism of attenuation geometrically.

$$\left|H(j\omega)\right|=\frac{\omega_c}{d}$$
*This formula reads: gain equals \(\omega_c\) divided by the distance from the pole to the point \(j\omega\).

- \(d\) is the geometric distance from the pole at \(-\omega_c\) to the evaluation point \(j\omega\) on the imaginary axis, exactly as shown in Fig. 4.50a.
- \(\omega_c\) is the fixed cutoff parameter in the numerator.

**When to use it:** When interpreting lowpass magnitude directly from a pole-zero plot.

**Exam trigger:** A question asking why gain decreases as \(\omega\) increases — answer using the growing distance \(d\).

**Common misuse:** Treating \(d\) as a constant. It is a frequency-dependent distance that grows as \(\omega\) increases.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Ideal lowpass needs a wall of poles

An ideal lowpass filter has gain exactly 1 from \(0\) to \(\omega_c\), then gain exactly 0 for all \(\omega > \omega_c\). This is the rectangular target shown in Fig. 4.50.

Because a pole enhances gain near the frequency it faces, maintaining uniform enhanced gain across the entire passband would require a pole facing every frequency in that band — plus conjugate poles for the negative-frequency side. The textbook describes this idealized picture as a **continuous wall of poles** lining the imaginary axis from \(-j\omega_c\) to \(+j\omega_c\).

**Practical compromise:** Real filters use a finite number \(N\) of poles. This gives a non-ideal but usable response. As \(N \to \infty\), the response approaches the ideal rectangular shape.

**Butterworth filters** are the family that achieves the maximally flat passband response for a given \(N\), as discussed in this section. They place their \(N\) poles on a semicircle in the left half-plane.

$$\left|H_{\mathrm{ideal}}(j\omega)\right|=\begin{cases}1, & 0\leq \omega\leq \omega_c\\0, & \omega>\omega_c\end{cases}$$
*This piecewise formula expresses the ideal lowpass target: unity gain through the passband and zero gain after the cutoff.

- \(\omega_c\) is the cutoff frequency — the boundary between passband and stopband.
- The ideal response has a perfectly vertical drop at \(\omega_c\), which no finite-order filter can replicate.

**When to use it:** When comparing a real lowpass filter to the ideal target.

**Exam trigger:** A shaded rectangular ideal response in a figure, or a question asking why finite filters cannot match it exactly.

**Common misuse:** Assuming a finite-order Butterworth filter achieves this sudden vertical drop. It does not — the drop is gradual and steepens only as \(N\) increases.*

$$N\to\infty\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{response approaches the ideal lowpass shape}$$
*\(N\) is the finite number of poles used in a practical Butterworth filter.

Increasing \(N\) makes the transition from passband to stopband sharper, but only the limiting case \(N \to \infty\) would achieve the ideal rectangular response.

**Common misuse:** Claiming that \(N=5\) or \(N=10\) is already ideal because the curve looks steep. The curve is steeper than \(N=1\), but it still has a gradual transition — not a vertical wall.*

## 4. Butterworth versus Chebyshev: what the textbook wants you to remember

**Butterworth filters** use the maximally flat passband idea. The textbook footnote states that for an order-\(N\) Butterworth response, the first \(2N-1\) derivatives of \(|H(j\omega)|\) with respect to \(\omega\) are zero at \(\omega=0\). This means the passband is as flat as possible near dc for that order.

**Chebyshev filters** use a semielliptical pole-wall shape instead of a semicircle. Their passband has ripple — the gain oscillates slightly rather than staying flat. However, this trade-off buys a faster drop in the stopband compared to a Butterworth filter of the same order.

### EXAM TIP

- **Flat passband emphasized?** Choose Butterworth.
- **Faster stopband drop with ripple allowed?** Choose Chebyshev.

These two descriptors are the primary exam-facing distinction between the two families.

$$\left.\frac{d^k}{d\omega^k}\left|H(j\omega)\right|\right|_{\omega=0}=0,\quad k=1,2,\ldots,2N-1$$
*This compactly states the textbook footnote defining maximally flat behavior for a Butterworth filter of order \(N\).

- \(N\) is the filter order.
- \(k\) is the derivative order, ranging from 1 to \(2N-1\).
- All derivatives up to order \(2N-1\) vanish at \(\omega=0\), meaning the magnitude response is as flat as possible near dc.

**When to use it:** When explaining what 'maximally flat' means near \(\omega=0\) for a Butterworth filter.

**Common misuse:** Confusing flat passband with faster stopband attenuation. Chebyshev filters have the faster stopband drop, but they achieve it by allowing passband ripple — the opposite of maximally flat.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **First-order lowpass transfer function:** \(H(s)=\dfrac{\omega_c}{s+\omega_c}\) — pole at \(-\omega_c\) on the negative real axis; numerator \(\omega_c\) ensures \(H(0)=1\).
- **DC gain normalization:** \(H(0)=1\) — always verify the numerator matches the pole location before assuming unity dc gain.
- **Magnitude as distance:** \(|H(j\omega)|=\dfrac{\omega_c}{d}\) — the pole is fixed; the evaluation point \(j\omega\) moves up the imaginary axis as \(\omega\) increases, growing \(d\) and shrinking the gain.
- **Ideal lowpass target:** \(|H_{\mathrm{ideal}}(j\omega)|=1\) for \(0\leq\omega\leq\omega_c\) and \(0\) for \(\omega>\omega_c\) — requires a continuous wall of poles; no finite-order filter achieves this exactly.
- **Effect of order N:** Increasing \(N\) makes the Butterworth response approach the ideal lowpass shape; \(N\to\infty\) is the only true ideal.
- **Maximally flat condition:** \(\left.\dfrac{d^k}{d\omega^k}|H(j\omega)|\right|_{\omega=0}=0\) for \(k=1,\ldots,2N-1\) — defines Butterworth; Chebyshev trades this flatness for faster stopband drop at the cost of passband ripple.

*Next, you will use the same pole-zero placement idea to understand other filter shapes such as notch filters.*
